title: Chestnut and sausagemeat stuffing
subtitle: Roasted chestnuts, pork sausagemeat, and herbs
excerpt: Pork sausagemeat combined with roasted chestnuts, onion, sage, and parsley, baked in a loaf tin until crisp on the outside and dense and flavourful within. The quintessential British Christmas stuffing.
sourceNotes: Chestnut stuffing is a longstanding British Christmas tradition, documented in Victorian cookery and established as a Christmas standard by the early twentieth century. Delia Smith's Complete Cookery Course (1978-82) includes a chestnut and sausagemeat stuffing as the standard British Christmas stuffing recipe.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before or after baking for up to 3 months.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Make in two tins.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make and refrigerate up to 2 days ahead. Bake from cold adding 10 minutes.

content.text: Chestnut and sausagemeat stuffing is the version that most British families make at Christmas, and for good reason: the chestnuts add sweetness and texture, the sausagemeat holds the stuffing together and provides richness, and sage is the herb that makes it taste of the season. Cooked outside the bird in a loaf tin, it can be sliced cleanly and serves as many portions as you need.

Use vacuum-packed cooked chestnuts rather than dried or canned. They are available year-round, have a clean, sweet flavour, and require no soaking or preparation. Roughly chopping them gives the stuffing texture; mashing them into the sausagemeat gives it body.

Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over medium-low heat. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion (and celery if using) for 8 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the sage, thyme, and parsley and cook for 1 minute. Remove from the heat and leave to cool.

Preheat the oven to 190°C fan. In a large bowl, combine {{sausagemeat}} of sausagemeat, {{chestnuts-cooked}} of roughly chopped chestnuts, the cooked onion mixture, {{breadcrumbs-fresh}} of fresh breadcrumbs, and the beaten egg. Season generously. Mix thoroughly — using your hands is the most effective method.

Pack into a buttered loaf tin and smooth the top. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes until golden brown on top and cooked through — a skewer inserted into the centre should come out hot. Rest for 5 minutes before slicing.

items.cause: Not enough binding — egg or sausagemeat was insufficient
items.fix: Add an extra beaten egg to the mixture next time. The sausagemeat and egg together provide the binding; without both the stuffing will not hold its shape.

items.cause: Too little breadcrumb or the mixture was overworked
items.fix: Add more breadcrumbs and mix only until just combined — like meatballs, overworked stuffing becomes dense.

items.cause: Loaf tin has high sides preventing direct heat
items.fix: Raise the tin to the upper third of the oven for the last 10 minutes, or flash under the grill briefly at the end.

content.text: Stuffing has been made in Britain since the mediaeval period, when it was used both to fill birds and to extend more expensive ingredients. The chestnut version became established as the Christmas stuffing in the twentieth century, following the tradition of roasting chestnuts that ran parallel to the cooking of the Christmas meal. Cooked stuffing baked outside the bird (rather than stuffed in the cavity) produces a more even result and allows the bird to cook faster. Delia Smith popularised this method and the specific chestnut-and-sausagemeat combination in her Complete Cookery Course; her version remains the benchmark.
